name: Ploceidae
description: Weavers are a large family of passerine birds known for their elaborately woven nests. They are primarily found in sub-Saharan Africa, with a few species occurring in Asia, and are named for their remarkable nest-building behavior.

children.name: Ploceus
children.description: A large genus of weavers with stout conical bills, known for their bright breeding plumages and woven nests.

children.name: Ploceus cucullatus
children.description: The Village Weaver is a common bird with black head and yellow body in breeding males. It is found across sub-Saharan Africa, building large woven nests in colonies near water.

children.name: Ploceus philippinus
children.description: The Baya Weaver is a sparrow-sized bird with bright yellow crown and breast in breeding males. It is famous for its intricately woven retort-shaped nests, built in colonies across South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Ploceus velatus
children.description: The Southern Masked Weaver has a black face and throat with bright yellow body in breeding males. It is common across southern Africa, weaving spherical nests in trees and reed beds.

children.name: Ploceus spekei
children.description: Speke's Weaver is a yellow weaver with a black face, throat, and bill in breeding males. It is endemic to the Horn of Africa, inhabiting savannas and building woven nests in acacia trees.

children.name: Ploceus nigerrimus
children.description: Vieillot's Black Weaver is a sexually dimorphic species: the male is glossy black with a pale eye, while the female is olive-green with streaked underparts. It inhabits forests and woodlands across equatorial Africa.

children.name: Ploceus nigricollis
children.description: The Black-necked Weaver has a black throat and nape, bright yellow body, and olive-green crown. It is found in woodlands and forest edges across much of sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Ploceus subaureus
children.description: The Golden Weaver is a bright yellow bird with an orange-red eye and olive-tinted back. It inhabits coastal wetlands and riverine vegetation of eastern and southern Africa.

children.name: Ploceus xanthops
children.description: Holub's Golden Weaver is a large, heavy-billed weaver with bright yellow plumage and a greenish wash on the back. It is found in marshes and moist grasslands of central and southern Africa.

children.name: Ploceus aurantius
children.description: The Orange Weaver has a brilliant orange-yellow body with a black face and throat in breeding males. It frequents coastal mangroves and swamps of western and central Africa.

children.name: Ploceus rubiginosus
children.description: The Chestnut Weaver has a rich chestnut body with a black face and dark eye in breeding males. It inhabits dry savannas and scrublands of eastern and southern Africa.

children.name: Ploceus intermedius
children.description: The Lesser Masked Weaver has a black face mask, bright yellow body, and pale eye in breeding males. It is a colonial nester found in woodlands and savannas of eastern and southern Africa.

children.name: Quelea
children.description: A genus of small weavers that form some of the largest bird flocks on Earth.

children.name: Quelea quelea
children.description: The Red-billed Quelea is a small weaver with a distinctive red bill and variable breeding plumage. It forms enormous flocks that can number in the millions, and is considered a major agricultural pest in Africa.

children.name: Quelea erythrops
children.description: The Red-headed Quelea has a bright red head and breast in breeding males, with a brown body and pale bill. It inhabits moist savannas and grasslands of western and central Africa.

children.name: Quelea cardinalis
children.description: The Cardinal Quelea has a rich crimson-red head and breast, brown back, and pinkish-red bill in breeding males. It is found in grassy wetlands of eastern and central Africa.

children.name: Amblyospiza
children.description: A monotypic genus of large, thick-billed weaver found in African wetlands and forests.

children.name: Amblyospiza albifrons
children.description: The Grosbeak Weaver is a large, robust weaver with a massive conical bill and a white forehead patch. It inhabits reed beds and forest edges across sub-Saharan Africa, building large woven nests.

children.name: Malimbus
children.description: A genus of forest-dwelling weavers with bright red and black plumage, found in tropical Africa.

children.name: Malimbus nitens
children.description: The Blue-billed Malimbe has a glossy black body with a bright red crown, throat, and breast, and a distinctive pale blue bill. It inhabits lowland rainforests of western and central Africa.

children.name: Malimbus rubricollis
children.description: The Red-headed Malimbe has a black body with a bright red head, throat, and upper breast. It is a canopy-dwelling weaver of lowland rainforests across equatorial Africa.

children.name: Euplectes
children.description: A genus of weavers known as widowbirds and bishop birds, with males exhibiting striking elongated tail feathers and bright breeding plumage.

children.name: Euplectes ardens
children.description: The Red-collared Widowbird has a black body with a vivid red band across the throat and a long, flowing tail in breeding males. It inhabits moist grasslands and marshes of eastern and southern Africa.

children.name: Euplectes macroura
children.description: The Yellow-mantled Widowbird has a black body with a bright yellow mantle and shoulder patch, and a broad fan-shaped tail in breeding males. It is found in wet grasslands of central and eastern Africa.

children.name: Euplectes axillaris
children.description: The Fan-tailed Widowbird has a black body with orange-red shoulder patches and a short, broad tail in breeding males. It is common in marshes and rank grasslands across much of sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Plocepasser
children.description: A genus of social weavers that build large, compartmentalized communal nests, found in savannas of eastern and southern Africa.

children.name: Plocepasser mahali
children.description: The White-browed Sparrow-Weaver has a brown body, white eyebrow, and white wing patches. It builds large, messy communal nests of grass in acacia trees, occupied year-round by social groups.
